original_abstract: Modulating the surface strain of nanoparticles (NPs)—the compression or expansion of surface atoms—is a powerful strategy for tuning surface chemistry and optimizing catalytic performance. In this study, we demonstrate that the surface strain of Pt in core/shell FePt/Pt NPs (featuring three atomic layers of Pt) can be precisely controlled by inducing a structural phase transition in the core from a cubic solid solution (fcc) to a tetragonal intermetallic (fct) structure. Quantum mechanics-molecular mechanics (QM-MM) simulations suggest that the significantly enhanced activity of fct-FePt/Pt NPs toward the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) stems from the relaxation of overcompressed Pt strain facilitated by the fct-FePt core. We further optimized this strain effect by partially substituting Fe with Cu; the resulting fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs emerged as an exceptionally efficient catalyst, delivering a specific activity nearly ten times higher than that of commercial Pt. This approach of structure-induced surface strain control provides a versatile platform for engineering highly efficient catalysts for the ORR and a broad range of other chemical transformations.
humanized_abstract: Control of surface strain in catalysts by inducing structural changes in metallic cores is highly desirable but rarely realized in practical catalytic systems. Here, we show that the surface strain of Pt in a Pt shell core/shell FePt NP structure can be controlled while the same NP structure is preserved, where we obtain 3 Pt layers with different surface strains. Through QM-MM calculations, we confirmed that high ORR performance of fct-FePt NPs is caused by relieving the over-compression strain in the Pt surface layers, which was induced by the large lattice mismatch between the fcc FePt and Pt. By tuning the lattice mismatch between FePt and Pt shells by partially replacing Fe by Cu in fct-FeCuPt/Pt, we finally obtained an extremely high specific activity towards the ORR, which is about 10 times as large as that of commercial Pt catalysts. The present structure-induced surface strain control method will open new avenues to design high-performance catalysts for the ORR as well as other catalytic reactions.

By tuning the lattice mismatch between FePt and Pt shells by partially replacing Fe by Cu in fct-FeCuPt/Pt, we finally obtained an extremely high specific activity towards the ORR, which is about 10 times as large as that of commercial Pt catalysts. The present structure-induced surface strain control method will open new avenues to design high-performance catalysts for the ORR as well as other catalytic reactions.
